summary: I always dreamed of going back to Somalia since I left when I was very young. In 2012, I finally returned and experienced a lot of cultural shock, especially with the living conditions and kitchen styles. Later, I got an internship with a research institute there as part of my graduate program, which really changed my perception of Somali society and helped me understand the country better. Somalia holds a special place in my heart because it gave me a sense of belonging and identity.

My first visit to Mogadishu was in 2012 when I went with my mom and stayed at our grandma's house, capturing many family photos. I returned again in March 2013 with my father, who hadn't been back since 1991, and it was special to see him reconnect with his family and show me his old neighborhood. Taking photos in Mogadishu was sometimes challenging because people were upset by it, but over time, with smartphones and iPads, photography has become more accepted. Mogadishu is where I began to deeply connect with my roots and family history.

During my 2013 trip to Mogadishu, I used my Canon camera to take incredible photos, including the moment my father reunited with his best friend after over twenty years. The Canon camera allowed me to capture important memories and emotions during that special visit. It was my tool to document the streets, buildings, and family moments despite some people's discomfort with photography at the time.

On my later trip to Somalia for an internship, I used my small pink iPad to take pictures, which helped me document my experience and the working life of Somalia. The iPad, a product from Apple Inc., became an important device for me to capture moments and understand my identity. I noticed how photography has evolved with devices like iPads and smartphones, making it a part of everyday life both in the U.S. and Somalia.
